title: Arrays & Loops
description: Arrays are the most common data structure in coding interviews. Master declaration, mutation, slicing, spreading, and every loop style JavaScript offers.

sections.title: Declare and access arrays
sections.explanation: Declare arrays with `[]` or `Array(n).fill(val)` for pre-sized arrays. Access by index in O(1). Negative indices don't work natively — use `arr.at(-1)` for the last element. Interviewers expect you to know that out-of-bounds access returns `undefined`, not an error.

exercise.prompt: Create an array `nums` with values [5, 10, 15, 20]. Print the first element, the last element using .at(-1), and the length.

validation.message: First element is 5, last is 20, length is 4
Use .at(-1) for the last element and .length for size

exercise.hint: Index 0 for first, .at(-1) for last, .length for size.

sections.title: push, pop, shift, unshift
sections.explanation: push/pop operate on the end in O(1). shift/unshift operate on the front in O(n) because elements must be reindexed. In interviews, prefer push/pop when possible. Know that pop() and shift() return the removed element.

exercise.prompt: Start with `arr = [10, 20, 30]`. Push 40, then pop the last element and store it. Print the popped value, then print the array. Then shift the first element and print it.

validation.message: Push 40 then pop gets 40 back; shift removes the front element 10
Use push, pop, and shift array methods

exercise.hint: push(40), then pop() returns 40, then shift() returns the first element 10.

sections.title: slice and spread operator
sections.explanation: slice(start, end) returns a shallow copy from start to end (exclusive) without modifying the original — essential for interview problems requiring subarrays. The spread operator `...` copies arrays and merges them. Both are O(n).

exercise.prompt: Given `nums = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]`, create a slice from index 1 to 4 and print it joined by commas. Then create a copy using spread and print its length.

validation.message: slice(1,4) gives [20,30,40], spread copy has length 5
Use slice for the subarray and spread for the copy

exercise.hint: slice(1, 4) for indices 1-3, and [...nums] for the copy.

sections.title: for, for...of, and forEach
sections.explanation: Use a classic `for` loop when you need the index (two-pointer, sliding window). Use `for...of` for clean iteration over values. Use `forEach` for side-effect-only passes. Avoid `for...in` on arrays — it iterates over string keys and includes inherited properties.

exercise.prompt: Given `nums = [2, 4, 6, 8]`, use a for...of loop to compute the sum and print it. Then use forEach to print each value doubled, each on its own line.

validation.message: Sum is 20, then each value doubled: 4, 8, 12, 16
Use for...of for the sum and forEach for the doubling

exercise.hint: Use 'of' in the for...of loop, and multiply by 2 in forEach.
